questions.prompt: What is the spread in a stock's order book?

choices.text: The gap between the lowest ask (cheapest sell order) and the highest bid (most aggressive buy order). It's the implicit cost of an instant round-trip: buying at the ask, then immediately selling at the bid, you lose the spread. Liquid names like AAPL trade at a $0.01 spread; thin small-caps may trade at $0.10+; an illiquid crypto pair might show 1%+. Spread is the first cost any backtest must include — strategies that ignore it perform much better on paper than in reality, especially on small-size or low-liquidity instruments.

questions.explanation: Spread = best ask − best bid. It's the implicit per-round-trip cost; thin names have wide spreads.

questions.prompt: Your backtest looks profitable using last-trade prices. Live trading loses money. Why?

choices.text: Last-trade prices aren't tradeable. To buy now you must lift the ask; to sell now you hit the bid — each round-trip costs the spread plus slippage as size moves the book. A backtest using mid-prices or last-trade massively overstates returns: a strategy that 'made $0.05 per trade' against last-trade may actually pay $0.02 spread + $0.01 slippage = $0.03 round-trip cost, leaving $0.02 — but the backtest didn't subtract any of that. Always model bid/ask fills (or last + spread + slippage) before believing the equity curve. This is the #1 cause of strategies that 'work' on paper and bleed live.

questions.explanation: Last-trade prices aren't tradeable. Model bid/ask fills and slippage or backtests overstate.
